speaker: interviewer
text: It's 1996. The survivor is Mr. Sol Wexler, interviewer Miriam Rabner, Tape 4. When you traveled and you got--

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm sorry. I think you made a mistake there about something. Can you read back those--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was it about?

speaker: interviewee
text: You just read something about the timing. Do you have today's date?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. I thought you didn't say in '96. I'm sorry.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's OK. When you traveled, and you traveled into Poland and then through Poland, how did you travel? On trains?

speaker: interviewee
text: On trains.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have papers?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We were traveling on top of trains, changing trains. It was a whole-- riga-- you know, we left Borszczow around April or May of '44. And then I get in the American zone till December '45. A trip that should take two days. Many times, I would fall asleep on the-- on the train. All of a sudden, I'm back where I was, turn around. Didn't have no water to clean ourselves or anything. Anyway, somehow I made myself-- I got to Munich. And they put us through an encampment. Not an encampment. We were in Deutsches Museum. It was a museum before. Then they made this place person camp out of it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And December was bitter cold, and I had no clothes. And I didn't know what to do. And I see a group of children came on the truck, and I go over to them. I said, where do you come from? So they tell me they're coming from an orphanage. What are you doing here? Here, we are going to a movie. It's like you would tell me you're going to the moon. OK. I ask, can I come with you? He says, no, we are all filled up. There's no train going back. You will know where you are. Don't go. I decided anyways I'm going to go. Truck came, and I started on the truck, and I hear the same story. That couldn't be, so many people saying don't go. Who am I? And I start climbing down the truck. And a guy grabs me by the shoulder, and he says to me, don't worry, you go. I just came back from looking for my parents in Germany.

speaker: interviewee
text: And these guys told me, it's OK. They're only afraid. They'll have less if I go there. So I climb up on the truck. And the first thing I do when I come into the office, first we go through Dachau. I didn't know what the kids were doing. They were collecting stones and putting like this. And when we came to Dachau, those stones started flying into the windows, while we were on the truck.

speaker: interviewee
text: We come through Indersdorf. I run into the office with my packages. I said, you can't throw me out. My father is an American citizen. There was a [NON-ENGLISH]. Who's taking you out? We are here to help you. Who told you such a stupid story? I'm not going to say nothing. And they took us for a bath. This was the first bath that I had in a year's time. Our clothes walked away by itself up here. And then they take us to a dining room. And there are tables of 10. I don't know anyone, so I said, is there an empty table? And I see they go over the place where they got food.

speaker: interviewee
text: I go with them. They take out the food and bring the food. They both sit down. We finish the food. And then they put us to bed. And this fellow that just called me was the one that told me not to go. We are best friends since that time. He was best man for my wedding here, three brothers. I was best man for his wedding. He has one of the highest positions for [INAUDIBLE] foods. Now that's a success story. But we remained friends through all this time. And the ironic part was he came to the stage before me, because I could come to the stage without a visa. And if I take that spot, I take somebody else's away. So I had to wait about four months longer than the children there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Which was frustrating, but all my friends went to visit my father.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the states?

speaker: interviewer
text: No. Where did you wait?

speaker: interviewee
text: I worked-- called the place [NON-ENGLISH]. They gave me a job. I was going to the American Council, where the people were supposed to immigrate. Emigrate, rather. And Hella remembers me taking her to the council. She remembered me taking her. [DRINKS]

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you meet Hella?

speaker: interviewee
text: Around 1949.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were already in--

speaker: interviewee
text: I came here in July of '46.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me how you came here.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is another Hella story. All my hopes were, when I come to the States, my life will change. I'll have my father that will take care of me with everything. And I build myself up, high expectations. And I come down the-- the ship. My father and my aunt jump on me, they start kissing me. And they take me to my aunt's house. She lived at 49 West 72nd Street. Didn't have money. And I come up. There is three cots for us to sleep. Oh, he said, it's OK. The next day, he takes me to his house. He's a boarder by somebody.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your dad?

speaker: interviewee
text: My-- my dad. He's got one room as big as that room, with two baths. He cannot-- no air conditioning, no refrigerator, nothing. I was ready to turn around. I had better in Germany than here. In the morning, my father wakes up. I see he's getting dressed. I said, where are you going? He says, I'm sorry, but I have to go to work. I says, can't take off one day? He says, no, we're in season. We cannot take-- I don't speak English. He says, don't worry. Go to this cafeteria. I'll pay him. Just what you want, ask him.

speaker: interviewer
text: So for three months, I-- this, this, this. You know what I mean? I pointed with my finger. Where did your father live? In--

speaker: interviewee
text: Brighton Beach. [DRINKS] And I don't know what happened. I couldn't say 'father.' For all the time, I addressed them in the third person. I just couldn't. The only time when I heard that he has cancer, I got very-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I tried to get very close to him. I was married already, but I stayed there all the time. And this was the second time I cried after my mother's--

speaker: interviewer
text: Murder?

speaker: interviewee
text: After my mother's death. I don't know. I couldn't cry when Hella died. I don't know. I don't know what it is. I feel different ways. [DRINKS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you not get close to your father because you felt he betrayed you by leaving?

speaker: interviewer
text: Or what was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: It could be. It could be. I had no reason, just emotion.

speaker: interviewer
text: You hadn't seen him for eight years.

speaker: interviewee
text: I know. So he was a complete stranger to me. I know he loved me, and I know he was trying very hard. And yet for some reason-- and I used to feel bad. I used to want to say it, and I just couldn't say it. It's like a sickness.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your father ever marry?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. My father remarried in-- [INAUDIBLE] 1950. Hella and I were supposed to-- he got married in '46, by the way. '48, I'm sorry.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who got married in '48?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your father.

speaker: interviewee
text: Father.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when you went and how did you meet Hella?

speaker: interviewee
text: I met Hella-- believe it or not, we used to have a group on Brighton Beach, Bay Five. That most of the European weddings happened through that beach. We also had a social club. But I met her on the beach, actually. A funny part I want to tell you, we were supposed to get married in-- in June. And my mother-- my stepmother got pregnant. And I couldn't walk down-- we couldn't walk down the aisle with her having a stomach. So we postponed it until December.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you had a half brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I had to half brother, right. He was closer to my children than to me, because he was more their age. And sort of the drifted away. I tried. He said-- he said he doesn't remember my father. He was-- I'm surprised at that. I remember since the age of five, he-- I sent him a video to show who his father is.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever go back to school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: In America?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't have no schooling or nothing in '41. So I had-- they put-- I had to go to public-- finish public school first. But after one year of public school, they were teaching in public school what I knew from public school from-- they were teaching in high school what I knew in public school. And it came very easy to me. So I went to Erasmus Hall, to be exactly. And I was a good student. And then I got-- I went about two years to Baruch, I think. Not Baruch, I'm sorry. Brooklyn College. And then I went-- I worked for somebody in the leather goods business for about 15 years wasted. Then I opened up a restaurant. And it was too hard. I didn't know the first thing of being a restaurant. I built it without knowing anything. I was doing pretty good. But getting up 3 o'clock in the morning, going home 10 o'clock at night, and not working Sunday. We didn't work. We just cleaned. I said, that's not for me. We sold it, and I went back to school. I went back to a special school for stockbrokers, financial, and I became a stockbroker. I was at that for 10 years. And my friend was in court, the surrogate judge. And I was-- we were very close. I told him, [INAUDIBLE], if you find something, keep me in mind. And he got me this job. I there to close to 20 years.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were you doing there?

speaker: interviewee
text: What I was doing there? I was in charge of all the money that come in. And besides this, we also had adoptions and guardianships. There was a lot of money there. And after everything was through, they had to come to me to give them the pass to collect the money.

speaker: interviewee
text: You must have heard of my judge. One that had a scandal, that they removed from the bench-- [INAUDIBLE]. You remember that? No? OK. It's a long story. And those go through this. And so that was the job from which you retired in the end?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the job from what I retired. And thank God I took that job. Otherwise, I don't know what I would have done. The sickness costed close to a million dollars. Luckily, I didn't have to spend too much. I certainly have other money, but-- and not once did Hella complain.

speaker: interviewer
text: You also had children. You forgot to mention them. You worked and worked and worked, [INTERPOSING VOICES] but you also had children. Tell me about your children.

speaker: interviewee
text: My children, let's see.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is older? Start with the oldest.

speaker: interviewee
text: Linda. Linda's older. She went through rough times with us. It was very bad without a job. If it wouldn't have been that Hella worked all the time. We wouldn't have been able to do it. But Linda, now she graduated from-- What do you call? From Lehman College. She's a CPA. And my son graduated from Baruch. And he's running one of the biggest food operations in the States.

words.text: If it wouldn't have been that

speaker: interviewer
text: And what's his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Michael. I'll give you an example. You don't hear this very much from children. By accident, Hella called the office instead of calling him. And the owner got on the phone, and he said, one thing I gotta tell you, Mrs. Wexler, there's no other Michael. I love him like my own brother. He's a [INAUDIBLE]. He says, he came to the cemetery. He came here, leaving a big business, he and his brother. And Michael is doing very good. He's got two children.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about your grandchild.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's another thing. My grandchildren are-- all children are good. I'm sending Jennifer for two months to Israel now with a group. And she's going to meet her parents, after she's through with the group, to visit other family. We have a lot of family there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Jennifer is your daughter Linda's daughter?

speaker: interviewee
text: Linda.

speaker: interviewer
text: You can't get up yet. We'll bring the pictures--

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm sorry.

speaker: interviewer
text: --in a few minutes. And does Linda have another child?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Has another child, Mark, who's going to get a Bar Mitzvah-- not Mark. See how good I am after I get tired? Mark.

speaker: interviewer
text: Joseph?

speaker: interviewee
text: There's a Mark too. Linda has Mark, and-- Linda has Mark and Jennifer, and Michael has Joseph and Ilana.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So you have four grandchildren?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have four grand--

speaker: interviewer
text: And you're close with them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very close. Funny thing is, I was able to be closer to the children than to my own children. And these kids are so good. I'll just give you an example. He was working. And at about 9:00 in the morning, the door opens up, and he and his boss come around again. Are you OK? What's the matter? I forgot to take up the receiver. And they were worried what happened to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: To put the receiver back.

speaker: interviewee
text: Receiver back. It was searching on this receiver. And they're supportive in such a way that I never believed a kid could be that way.

speaker: interviewer
text: You're very lucky.

speaker: interviewee
text: I am. And I have an open house with all the heart. I was lucky with everybody here. I have a cousin that's just like a sister. They wouldn't let me stay home for a minute. It's time. I said, yes, time.

speaker: interviewee
text: Something interesting. My mother was killed April 7. My daughter was born April 7. Two of my children were born in April. Hella died in April. Coincidence? Very emotional month.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Either something good or something bad happens. And you know, they tell me that during April I was always very depressed, especially passover.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's just about the end of your story. And I wanted to ask you, is there a specific message that you want to give to children and grandchildren, and the generations to come, about the Holocaust or about being Jewish? About Israel? Anything that's important to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: They're very close, as far as Jewishness. My daughter-in-law, her father was Italian. I'm telling it to you for a reason. They never had any religious upbringing. And one day, Michael came, and we were in synagogue. And he was standing near the door. I saw him from [INAUDIBLE] came down, and says, you are a guest. Please sit down. And he did it twice in a row. And Michael says, we want-- I want that rabbi. He was teaching his wife, Jill, for three months without [INAUDIBLE]. He married them off. Jill never touched, have any religion or anything like this. She's in charge of the Judaica, and she is on the board of directors of the show. And [INAUDIBLE] school house of very, very, very active to the point-- she graduated from [INAUDIBLE] high school. That every time the rabbi goes away, she runs the services. I couldn't do any better with children.

words.text: I'm telling it to you for a reason.
he was standing near the door.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you've given them this connection to Judaism.

speaker: interviewee
text: I think Hella--

speaker: interviewer
text: And you think it's important for them to keep it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Absolutely. I mentioned to you I'm sending Linda-- I mean, Jennifer-- I always say-- when I want to say Jennifer, I say Linda. But they're very good. Very-- they have no problems at school, no problems with dope or anything like this. And Jennifer decided to be a singer. So she's taking singing lessons.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, that's what you call [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH]. That this thing wouldn't happen. That was-- Like I said before, she never cried at once. If she wasn't right, she wouldn't let us come until she fixes herself up. She had so many good friends. You know, we had over 500 people to the funeral. Everybody was there from A to Z.

speaker: interviewer
text: I thank you for telling me your story, for letting us into your home.

speaker: interviewee
text: I thank you. Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Into your life.

speaker: interviewee
text: I thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Who is in that picture? The couple? Are those your parents? We're showing the picture where there's a couple, right.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. You have two couples here. OK. Can I talk already?

speaker: interviewer
text: You talk, please. Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken, I think, when my mother was engaged. Mother and father were engaged, I think. She looks like about 18 here. Her mother name-- my mother's name was Lea [NON-ENGLISH] Wexler. Well And my father is Ira Wexler, known as Munia Weichselblatt.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your mother's maiden name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother's maiden name was Sternschuss.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Who is on that picture, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of my grandfather. His name was Weichselblatt. Jacov Weichselblatt. [NON-ENGLISH], the way I called him. And he was killed at this age. They were all hiding in a group. I took some other pictures, which I'll mention. And he was killed, it must have been in the '80s. They were killed in a very horrible way. They found them, and they cut their throats.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did the picture survive? How did you get to keep it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh. Some of these pictures, my father had. But some of these pictures, I always carried on me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Throughout the war? Throughout your hiding?

speaker: interviewee
text: Throughout the war. I didn't want to lose my father's address. I was afraid I'd lose this. And luckily, I had them. This picture was taken in 1938, a few days before my father left for the States Now from left to right, the first row, I'm in the corner there.

speaker: interviewer
text: The little boy is sitting.

speaker: interviewee
text: And next to me and my little-- my brother-- next to the cousin, Yossel was his name. Then another cousin, Mintzia. And I'm going from left to right again. My father is sitting on the left hand corner with a mustache there a little bit. Next to him is my uncle. And next to my uncle is my aunt. There they were married. Then goes my grandfather and my father's brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: You want from right to left, OK? That's-- on top, to the right, [BACKGROUND NOISE] is my aunt. These are children of my uncle. I don't remember the name. Isn't that funny? Oh, I know they're cousins. Those are old cousins in the last row standing?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the last row, they're all cousins.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is your mother on that picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, all the way in the corner on the left.

speaker: interviewer
text: She's standing behind your father.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. Now, left, this top--

speaker: interviewer
text: In the last row?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the last row in the back, him, he came together with my father to the States. He gave my father one visa and him one visa. And we are very close. Unfortunately, he lost a young son last year. Another interesting fact is Frank-- that's my cousin that came with my father-- his son called me up. And he says to me, Sol, I have an embarrassing thing to do. I says, what? I showed this picture to my father, and my father couldn't remember a single name of my cousins.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Who is in that picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken in 1946, when I came over to the States. My father-- that's my father and myself. My father was, at that time, 45 years old. And I was about 18 there. This is Hella and Sol. Their wedding picture. And all the surrounding pictures is all from the wedding.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was Hella's maiden name?

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH]. And my father-- I think this is-- it's engagement pictures, and the-- and the wedding.

speaker: interviewer
text: The wedding took place in New York?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And the paradise-- remember the paradise movie? In the bottom there, we were engaged already.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is on that picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: These are my maternal grandparents, Chaya Sternschuss and Shlomo or Sam Stern-- Wexler, I'm sorry.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sternschuss.

speaker: interviewee
text: Sternschuss. Did I louse it up now?

speaker: interviewer
text: No, that's OK. Don't worry about it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Shlomo Sternschuss has four first cousins. For every sister has-- a Shlomo.

speaker: interviewer
text: Everybody named after--

speaker: interviewee
text: After him. [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you know when this picture was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: Must have been taken someplace in the 16th or 17th, I don't know.

speaker: interviewee
text: He got killed in 18-- I think in the 18-- maybe that's a year before. I don't know. She was a little bit like my Aunt Zesta. She lived with us, and she practically brought me out by herself.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were you close to her?

speaker: interviewee
text: We slept in the same bed, because we didn't have where to sleep.

speaker: interviewer
text: I guess you were close.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And I think I was her favorite. I don't want to say it, but I think so. Because I grew up with her.
